competency_cluster: cluster_01: Body Awareness and Personal Boundaries
coconstruction_stages: Introduce the idea that we'll create a guide together about knowing our bodies and privacy; Have students share what they already know about body parts and which ones might be private; Work together to organize their ideas into different levels of understanding; Refine the levels by discussing what each one looks like in practice
coconstruction_student_prompts: What body parts can you name and how do you know which ones are private?; How would you explain to a friend which body parts should stay covered?; What does it look like when someone really understands body parts and privacy?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that show clear differences in naming accuracy and privacy understanding without being overly detailed or inappropriate for the age group.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet tried to name body parts or talk about privacy.
student_rubric_emerging: I can name some body parts with help but get confused about which ones are private.
student_rubric_developing: I can name most body parts on my own but sometimes mix up which areas are private.
student_rubric_competent: I can name body parts including private areas and clearly explain which parts are private.
student_rubric_extending: I can use my knowledge about body parts and privacy to explain boundaries in different situations.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I name the main body parts and explain which ones are private?; Am I able to explain privacy rules clearly to others?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Start with basic body part identification using simple pictures or diagrams; Introduce the concept that some body parts are private and should be covered
feedback_moves_emerging: Practice naming body parts with visual supports and gentle correction; Use simple language to clarify which areas are private and why
feedback_moves_developing: Review privacy concepts through role-play or discussion scenarios; Provide consistent reminders about which body parts are considered private
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge thinking by discussing privacy in different settings or contexts; Encourage explaining privacy concepts to help others understand

competency_cluster: cluster_01: Body Awareness and Personal Boundaries
coconstruction_stages: Discuss what boundaries mean and why they matter for feeling safe and comfortable; Brainstorm different types of boundaries we might need with our bodies and personal space; Explore how we can communicate boundaries clearly using words and actions; Practice identifying when boundaries are communicated well versus when they need improvement
coconstruction_student_prompts: What does it mean to have boundaries about your body and personal space?; How can we tell someone about our boundaries in a clear way?; What makes boundary communication work well in different situations?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that show clear progression from unclear boundary attempts to confident communication across various age-appropriate contexts.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet tried to communicate my boundaries about physical contact or privacy.
student_rubric_emerging: I can try to express my boundaries with help but my communication is unclear or inconsistent.
student_rubric_developing: I can communicate some boundaries about physical contact but struggle with privacy needs or being clear.
student_rubric_competent: I can clearly communicate my boundaries about both physical contact and privacy needs in appropriate situations.
student_rubric_extending: I can adapt my boundary communication effectively across different contexts and help others express their boundaries.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I clearly tell others about my boundaries for both physical contact and privacy?; Do I communicate my boundaries in ways that work well in different situations?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Model simple boundary statements and practice together; Create safe opportunities to express basic comfort preferences
feedback_moves_emerging: Help clarify specific boundary language and practice consistent messaging; Provide sentence starters for expressing boundaries clearly
feedback_moves_developing: Practice communicating privacy boundaries using the same clarity as physical contact boundaries; Role-play boundary communication in different scenarios
feedback_moves_competent: Explore how boundary communication changes across different contexts and relationships; Practice supporting peers in expressing their own boundaries

coconstruction_stages: Students brainstorm all the different types of families they know from their lives and communities.; Teacher guides students to group similar family types together and create category names.; Students discuss what makes each family type unique and special.; Class creates success criteria for describing family structures clearly and accurately.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What different kinds of families do you see in your neighborhood or school?; How would you group these families that are similar?; What makes each type of family special or unique?; What should we include when we describe a family structure well?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that represent the full range of family structures students identified, ensuring cultural diversity and avoiding stereotypes.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to identify different family structures.
student_rubric_emerging: I can name some family types with help but my descriptions need more work.
student_rubric_developing: I can identify several family forms on my own but my descriptions could be more detailed.
student_rubric_competent: I can independently identify and accurately describe various family structures including nuclear, single-parent, blended, extended, adoptive, and same-sex parent families.
student_rubric_extending: I can compare family structures across different cultures or explain how family diversity makes communities stronger.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I name at least six different types of family structures?; Are my descriptions accurate and detailed enough for someone else to understand each family type?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide visual examples of different family structures from books or photos.; Start with one familiar family type and guide student to describe what they notice.
feedback_moves_emerging: Ask student to choose one family type they named and help them add more details.; Provide sentence starters to help student describe family characteristics more completely.
feedback_moves_developing: Encourage student to add specific details about family roles or living arrangements.; Guide student to check their descriptions against real examples to ensure accuracy.
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge student to research family structures from different cultures or time periods.; Ask student to explain connections between family diversity and community strengths.

coconstruction_stages: Introduce the concept of safety and harm through student experiences; Explore different types of unsafe situations students might encounter; Develop criteria for recognising when situations become harmful; Create descriptors for different levels of recognition ability
coconstruction_student_prompts: What makes a situation feel unsafe to you?; How can we tell the difference between something uncomfortable and something harmful?; What clues help us recognise when someone needs help?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select scenarios that range from obvious safety concerns to more subtle warning signs, ensuring examples are age-appropriate and culturally relevant.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet shown I can identify abuse or unsafe situations.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some obvious forms of abuse with help but miss subtle warning signs.
student_rubric_developing: I can recognise clear forms of abuse on my own but struggle with complex unsafe situations.
student_rubric_competent: I can independently identify different forms of abuse and recognise when situations are unsafe or harmful.
student_rubric_extending: I can identify abuse patterns and unsafe situations across different contexts with detailed reasoning.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I explain why this situation is unsafe without help from others?; Am I able to spot warning signs even when they're not obvious?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide clear examples of safe versus unsafe situations; Use visual aids to highlight obvious warning signs
feedback_moves_emerging: Guide students to notice subtle indicators through questioning; Practice with scenarios that have less obvious warning signs
feedback_moves_developing: Present complex scenarios for analysis and discussion; Encourage reasoning about why situations might be harmful
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge with diverse contexts and cultural situations; Ask students to explain patterns they notice across different scenarios

coconstruction_stages: Students share what makes a good friend and identify friendship challenges they've experienced; Students brainstorm strategies for building friendships and handling conflicts; Students sort strategies from simple to complex and discuss when each might be used; Students create criteria for what friendship success looks like at different levels
coconstruction_student_prompts: What does it look like when someone is really good at making and keeping friends?; What strategies help when friendships get difficult or complicated?; How can you tell if your friendship strategies are working well?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that show clear progression from basic friendship behaviors to complex social navigation and peer support.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet tried to use strategies for building or maintaining friendships.
student_rubric_emerging: I can try some friendship strategies with help but don't always use them at the right time.
student_rubric_developing: I can use basic friendship strategies on my own but need help with complicated friendship problems.
student_rubric_competent: I can use effective strategies to build caring friendships and handle friendship challenges appropriately.
student_rubric_extending: I can use friendship strategies in many different situations and help others with their friendship challenges.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I handle friendship problems without always needing adult help?; Do I use different strategies depending on the friendship situation?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Model one simple friendship strategy and practice it together; Help identify one specific friendship goal to work toward
feedback_moves_emerging: Practice timing when to use specific friendship strategies; Provide prompts before social situations to remind of available strategies
feedback_moves_developing: Discuss how to adapt basic strategies for more complex situations; Practice problem-solving steps for unfamiliar friendship challenges
feedback_moves_competent: Explore how friendship strategies work in different social contexts; Create opportunities to mentor others with friendship skills

coconstruction_stages: Students share experiences or observations about unfair treatment to activate prior knowledge.; Guide students to categorize different types of harmful behaviors they've identified.; Students discuss and define what makes these behaviors harmful to individuals and groups.; Collaboratively create criteria for recognizing and explaining different types of bullying and stereotypes.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What are different ways people can be mean or unfair to others?; How do these behaviors affect the people who experience them?; What makes someone really good at spotting and explaining these problems?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select clear scenarios that show distinct bullying types and obvious stereotypes with visible harmful effects on individuals or groups.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to identify types of bullying or stereotypes.
student_rubric_emerging: I can name some types of bullying with help but my explanations need work.
student_rubric_developing: I can identify basic types of bullying and stereotypes but need to add more detail about their harmful impacts.
student_rubric_competent: I can identify different types of bullying and stereotypes and clearly explain how they cause harm.
student_rubric_extending: I can analyze complex bullying situations and connect stereotypes to bigger patterns of discrimination.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I name specific types of bullying and stereotypes without help?; Can I clearly explain how these behaviors harm people?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide concrete examples of bullying scenarios to analyze together.; Use visual aids or stories to help identify different harmful behaviors.
feedback_moves_emerging: Ask guiding questions to help elaborate on incomplete explanations.; Provide sentence starters for describing harmful impacts.
feedback_moves_developing: Prompt for specific details about how bullying affects victims emotionally and socially.; Encourage use of precise vocabulary to describe different stereotype categories.
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge to examine more complex scenarios with multiple bullying types.; Guide analysis of how stereotypes connect to larger social patterns.

competency_cluster: cluster_08: Online Safety and Digital Citizenship
lt_name: Understanding Digital Risks and Safety Principles
coconstruction_stages: Students brainstorm what could go wrong online from their own experiences.; Groups categorize risks and discuss what makes information personal or private.; Class develops safety rules for different online situations.; Students test safety principles against real-world scenarios.; Groups refine and finalize comprehensive safety guidelines.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What online experiences have made you feel unsafe or uncomfortable?; How do you decide what information is safe to share online?; What would you do if someone online asked for your personal details?; How can you tell if an online relationship is healthy or concerning?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select student work samples that show clear progression from basic risk awareness to comprehensive safety application across different digital contexts.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet identified online risks or explained safety principles.
student_rubric_emerging: I can name some online risks with help but my explanations need work.
student_rubric_developing: I can identify basic online risks and explain simple safety principles on my own.
student_rubric_competent: I can identify many different online risks and explain complete safety principles for protecting my information and relationships.
student_rubric_extending: I can apply safety principles to new situations and evaluate risks beyond typical online scenarios.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I explain why this online situation might be risky?; Do my safety principles cover both personal information and digital relationships?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide concrete examples of common online risks students encounter.; Guide students to reflect on their own digital experiences.
feedback_moves_emerging: Ask students to explain their thinking about specific risks.; Connect incomplete ideas to real-world consequences.
feedback_moves_developing: Challenge students to consider less obvious risks.; Push for more detailed explanations of safety principles.
feedback_moves_competent: Present novel digital scenarios for safety principle application.; Encourage evaluation of emerging technologies and their risks.

competency_cluster: cluster_08: Online Safety and Digital Citizenship
lt_name: Evaluating and Responding to Online Threats
coconstruction_stages: Students share experiences of feeling uncertain or uncomfortable online without naming specifics; Class brainstorms what makes online situations feel safe versus unsafe; Students work in groups to categorise different types of online threats from general to complex; Groups develop response strategies for each threat category; Class creates shared language for describing levels of threat evaluation skills
coconstruction_student_prompts: What are some warning signs that an online situation might not be safe?; How do you decide whether to respond, report, or remove yourself from an online situation?; What would it look like to help a friend recognise online threats?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select scenarios that progress from obvious scams to subtle manipulation tactics and include both relationship-based and content-based threats.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet shown that I can evaluate online threats or plan responses
student_rubric_emerging: I can spot obvious online risks with help but my responses need improvement
student_rubric_developing: I can recognise clear online threats on my own but find complex situations challenging
student_rubric_competent: I can evaluate online relationships and content critically and choose appropriate responses
student_rubric_extending: I can apply my evaluation skills to new online situations and help others recognise threats
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I explain why a specific online situation feels unsafe and what I would do about it?; Am I able to help others think through online safety decisions?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide concrete examples of online threats and walk through identification together; Use guided questions to help recognise one obvious warning sign
feedback_moves_emerging: Practice identifying threats in scenarios with decreasing support; Focus on one appropriate response strategy at a time
feedback_moves_developing: Present complex scenarios that require deeper analysis of multiple factors; Practice evaluating the effectiveness of different response options
feedback_moves_competent: Introduce novel online contexts that require transfer of existing skills; Provide opportunities to mentor others in threat recognition

coconstruction_stages: Discuss what safety concerns look like in different settings; Identify who we can turn to for help in various situations; Practice using clear words to describe problems; Explore different ways to report concerns based on the situation
coconstruction_student_prompts: What are some safety concerns you might encounter at school or home?; Who are the trusted adults you can talk to when you need help?; How can you explain a problem clearly so others understand?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that show different types of safety concerns and various appropriate reporting channels students might actually encounter.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet tried to report concerns or ask for help.
student_rubric_emerging: I can try to tell someone about safety concerns with help.
student_rubric_developing: I can recognize when I need help but sometimes use unclear words or tell the wrong person.
student_rubric_competent: I can report safety concerns and ask for help using clear words and telling the right person.
student_rubric_extending: I can adjust how I report concerns based on different situations and help others know how to seek help.
student_rubric_self_checks: Did I use clear words that help others understand my concern?; Did I choose the right person or place to report my concern?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Model simple ways to ask for help in safe situations; Practice identifying trusted adults together
feedback_moves_emerging: Provide sentence starters for reporting concerns; Help identify the most appropriate person to tell
feedback_moves_developing: Practice using specific vocabulary to describe different types of concerns; Review which channels work best for different situations
feedback_moves_competent: Present complex scenarios requiring adapted reporting strategies; Encourage peer mentoring opportunities

coconstruction_stages: Explore what consent and boundaries mean in different relationships; Identify what effective communication looks like in relationship scenarios; Examine how consent and boundaries apply in romantic and sexual contexts; Discuss how to support others with boundary understanding
coconstruction_student_prompts: What does it mean to respect someone's boundaries in a relationship?; How do we know when someone is giving clear consent?; What makes boundary communication challenging in different situations?; How can we help others understand consent and boundaries?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select scenarios that show clear progression from basic boundary recognition to complex relationship navigation and peer support situations.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to communicate consent or boundaries.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some consent concepts with support but communicate boundaries inconsistently.
student_rubric_developing: I can communicate basic consent and boundaries in familiar situations.
student_rubric_competent: I can independently recognise, respect and communicate consent and boundaries effectively in early romantic and sexual relationships.
student_rubric_extending: I can transfer consent communication skills to new contexts and support others in understanding boundary dynamics.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I clearly communicate my own boundaries and respect others' boundaries?; Do I understand consent in different relationship contexts?; Can I help others navigate consent and boundary situations?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide clear examples of what consent and boundaries look like in relationships; Practice identifying boundary statements in simple scenarios
feedback_moves_emerging: Focus on consistent language for expressing boundaries; Practice recognising consent cues in structured activities
feedback_moves_developing: Explore boundary communication in more complex relationship scenarios; Discuss how consent applies differently across various relationship contexts
feedback_moves_competent: Apply consent and boundary skills to unfamiliar or challenging situations; Practice supporting peers through boundary-related discussions

coconstruction_stages: Students brainstorm what makes health information trustworthy or untrustworthy.; Students examine different sexual health sources and discuss quality indicators.; Students practice identifying misinformation and develop counter-strategies.; Students create criteria for evaluating information and helping others.; Students refine rubric levels based on their evaluation experiences.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What clues tell you if sexual health information is reliable?; How would you help someone who believes misinformation?; What makes someone ready to teach others about information quality?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select diverse sexual health information sources ranging from clearly credible to obviously problematic, including social media posts and peer conversations that students commonly encounter.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to evaluate sexual health information.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some accurate information with help but struggle to counter misinformation effectively.
student_rubric_developing: I can evaluate basic sexual health information independently but inconsistently identify or address misinformation.
student_rubric_competent: I can independently evaluate medically accurate sexual health information and effectively counter misinformation with appropriate signposting.
student_rubric_extending: I can transfer evaluation skills to complex contexts and proactively educate others about misinformation.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I explain why this source is trustworthy or not?; Would I feel confident helping a friend evaluate sexual health information?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide a simple checklist for evaluating one sexual health source.; Model thinking aloud while evaluating information quality.
feedback_moves_emerging: Practice identifying red flags in misinformation with guided support.; Rehearse simple phrases for redirecting to accurate sources.
feedback_moves_developing: Compare multiple sources on the same topic to build consistency.; Practice addressing misinformation scenarios with peer feedback.
feedback_moves_competent: Apply evaluation skills to unfamiliar or complex sexual health topics.; Develop strategies for teaching evaluation skills to peers.

competency_cluster: cluster_15: Digital Wellbeing and Online Behaviour
coconstruction_stages: Students brainstorm what happens when they use digital devices and apps; Teacher guides sorting of student ideas into helpful and harmful categories; Students discuss how digital activities make them feel physically and emotionally; Class creates shared language for describing different levels of understanding about digital impacts
coconstruction_student_prompts: What are some things that happen when you spend time online or using apps?; How do these digital activities affect how you feel in your body and mind?; What makes someone really good at understanding digital risks and benefits?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose student work samples that show clear progression from basic naming of risks to detailed explanations of wellbeing impacts.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet tried to identify online risks or benefits
student_rubric_emerging: I can name some online risks or benefits with help
student_rubric_developing: I can identify basic online risks and benefits on my own
student_rubric_competent: I can identify various online risks and benefits and explain how they affect my wellbeing
student_rubric_extending: I can analyze complex digital situations and evaluate detailed impacts on different parts of wellbeing
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I explain how this digital activity affects my physical, emotional, or social wellbeing?; Am I identifying both risks and benefits rather than just one side?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide concrete examples of common online activities to analyze; Use visual prompts showing digital scenarios
feedback_moves_emerging: Ask students to explain their thinking about identified risks or benefits; Provide sentence starters for describing digital impacts
feedback_moves_developing: Guide students to connect digital activities to specific feelings or physical effects; Prompt reflection on personal experiences with digital wellbeing
feedback_moves_competent: Present complex scenarios with multiple digital elements to analyze; Encourage consideration of long-term and varied wellbeing impacts

coconstruction_stages: Brainstorm what we already know about staying healthy; Sort health knowledge into categories like nutrition, exercise, and hygiene; Define what makes health information accurate versus incomplete; Create quality levels from basic naming to explaining connections
coconstruction_student_prompts: What health facts do you think are most important to know?; How can we tell if our health knowledge is complete or has gaps?; What would it look like to connect different areas of health together?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select student work samples that clearly show progression from naming isolated health facts to demonstrating comprehensive understanding across all health domains.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to identify health facts
student_rubric_emerging: I can name some health facts with help but my understanding has errors
student_rubric_developing: I can identify basic health facts on my own but have gaps in nutrition or hygiene knowledge
student_rubric_competent: I can independently identify key facts about nutrition, physical activity, substance use, hygiene, and health maintenance practices accurately
student_rubric_extending: I can connect health facts across different areas and explain relationships between health practices
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I accurately name important facts in all five health areas without help?; Am I able to explain how different health practices connect to each other?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide visual health fact cards to sort and discuss; Start with one familiar health topic like handwashing
feedback_moves_emerging: Correct misconceptions immediately with accurate information; Use graphic organizers to organize health facts by category
feedback_moves_developing: Focus practice on the specific health domains showing gaps; Provide targeted resources for nutrition or hygiene knowledge building
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge to find connections between different health practices; Ask how one health choice affects other areas of wellness

competency_cluster: cluster_17: Healthcare Access and Medical Decision-Making
coconstruction_stages: Brainstorm different types of healthcare needs students or families might have; Identify various healthcare services that address these needs; Discuss how people find and access these services; Create criteria for matching appropriate services to specific needs; Develop language for describing access methods clearly
coconstruction_student_prompts: What healthcare needs have you or your family experienced?; How do people find the right healthcare service when they need help?; What makes one healthcare service more appropriate than another for a specific need?; What information would someone need to successfully access a healthcare service?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select examples that show clear progression from basic services with simple access to complex services requiring multiple steps or specialized knowledge.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to identify healthcare services or how to access them
student_rubric_emerging: I can name some healthcare services with help but my access information is incomplete
student_rubric_developing: I can identify basic healthcare services and explain simple ways to access them
student_rubric_competent: I can identify appropriate healthcare services for different needs and clearly explain how to access them
student_rubric_extending: I can evaluate healthcare service options and explain access strategies for complex healthcare needs
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I match the right type of healthcare service to specific needs?; Can I explain the steps someone would take to access the services I identified?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide a simple scenario and ask what type of healthcare help might be needed; Show examples of common healthcare services and discuss when they might be used
feedback_moves_emerging: Ask clarifying questions about the access steps they described; Provide additional information to fill gaps in their access explanations
feedback_moves_developing: Present more complex healthcare scenarios that require specialized services; Guide them to consider multiple access methods or steps for the same service
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge them to compare different service options for the same need; Ask them to consider barriers or complications in accessing specialized services

competency_cluster: cluster_18: Personal Safety and Risk Management
coconstruction_stages: Students brainstorm what makes something dangerous or unsafe in familiar places; Groups categorize hazards by how likely they are to happen and how serious the harm could be; Class discusses what it means to assess risk versus just spotting hazards; Students test their understanding by ranking hazards from different scenarios
coconstruction_student_prompts: What could hurt someone in this environment?; How likely is this hazard to actually cause harm?; Which hazard would you worry about most and why?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose scenarios from varied environments that show clear differences between spotting hazards and evaluating their actual risk levels.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to identify hazards or assess risks
student_rubric_emerging: I can spot obvious hazards with help but struggle to judge how risky they really are
student_rubric_developing: I can find common hazards on my own but my risk assessment needs more depth
student_rubric_competent: I can identify potential hazards in different places and accurately assess their safety risks
student_rubric_extending: I can systematically evaluate complex risk scenarios and rank multiple hazards by how severe and likely they are
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I explain why this hazard is more or less risky than others?; Have I considered both how likely the hazard is and how much harm it could cause?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Point out one obvious hazard and ask what could go wrong; Provide a simple checklist of things to look for
feedback_moves_emerging: Ask them to explain why one hazard is riskier than another; Guide them to consider both likelihood and severity
feedback_moves_developing: Challenge them to find less obvious hazards in the same environment; Have them justify their risk assessment with specific reasoning
feedback_moves_competent: Present complex scenarios with multiple interacting hazards; Ask them to create a priority ranking system for different types of risks

competency_cluster: cluster_18: Personal Safety and Risk Management
lt_name: Managing Personal Safety and Seeking Help
coconstruction_stages: Students brainstorm what safety means in different contexts and situations.; Students identify specific strategies they already use to stay safe.; Students discuss challenges with peer pressure and when help-seeking is needed.; Students organize strategies and help-seeking behaviors from basic to advanced levels.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What does staying safe look like in different places or situations?; When have you successfully handled peer pressure or asked for help?; What makes some safety strategies harder to use than others?; How could you help someone else learn to stay safe?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that show clear differences between basic safety awareness and effective implementation across various contexts.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet shown that I can use safety strategies or ask for help.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some safety strategies with help but find them hard to use effectively.
student_rubric_developing: I can use basic safety strategies on my own but sometimes struggle with peer pressure or asking for help.
student_rubric_competent: I can use appropriate safety strategies, handle peer pressure well, and ask for help when I need it.
student_rubric_extending: I can apply safety skills in new situations and help others develop their own safety strategies.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I name specific safety strategies I use and explain when to seek help?; How well do I handle situations where peers might influence my safety choices?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Help identify one specific safety strategy relevant to the student's daily life.; Practice recognizing trusted adults who can provide help.
feedback_moves_emerging: Guide practice applying identified strategies in low-risk scenarios.; Provide sentence starters for help-seeking conversations.
feedback_moves_developing: Role-play peer pressure scenarios with response strategies.; Create a personal help-seeking plan with specific steps and contacts.
feedback_moves_competent: Present novel safety scenarios requiring strategy adaptation.; Facilitate opportunities to mentor peers in safety planning.

coconstruction_stages: Brainstorm what students already know about physical changes during puberty; Discuss what makes a good explanation versus just listing changes; Sort student examples of physical changes into categories of explanation quality; Refine criteria for complete and accurate explanations
coconstruction_student_prompts: What physical changes do you think happen during puberty?; What's the difference between naming a change and explaining it well?; How would you know if someone really understands these changes?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select student work samples that show clear differences in explanation depth and accuracy rather than just listing physical changes.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to explain physical changes during puberty.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify some physical changes with help but my explanations need work.
student_rubric_developing: I can identify physical changes during puberty but my explanations could be more detailed.
student_rubric_competent: I can explain the physical changes that occur during puberty and adolescence accurately and completely.
student_rubric_extending: I can explain puberty changes and connect them to how timing and development vary between individuals.
student_rubric_self_checks: Did I explain the changes or just list them?; Are my explanations accurate and complete?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Provide sentence starters about one specific physical change; Use visual aids to prompt discussion of observable changes
feedback_moves_emerging: Ask students to elaborate on one change they mentioned; Provide accurate information to correct misconceptions
feedback_moves_developing: Prompt for more detail about how or why changes occur; Encourage connections between related physical changes
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge students to consider individual differences in timing; Ask them to connect physical changes to broader developmental patterns

coconstruction_stages: Students share experiences of recognizing when they or others needed health attention; Class discusses what makes health concerns obvious versus subtle; Students brainstorm different strategies people use to protect their wellbeing; Groups categorize health identification skills from simple to complex situations; Class agrees on what effective application of protective strategies looks like
coconstruction_student_prompts: When have you noticed someone needed health help and what made you realize it?; What's the difference between a health concern that's easy to spot and one that's harder to notice?; How do you know if a wellbeing strategy is working effectively?; What makes some health situations more complex than others?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that show clear progression from obvious to subtle health concerns and from single to multiple protective strategies.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to identify health issues or apply protective strategies
student_rubric_emerging: I can recognize obvious health concerns with help but apply strategies inaccurately
student_rubric_developing: I can identify clear health issues and apply basic strategies but miss subtle concerns
student_rubric_competent: I can identify when health issues require attention and apply appropriate strategies to protect wellbeing effectively
student_rubric_extending: I can transfer health identification skills to complex situations and integrate multiple protective strategies
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I spot both obvious and subtle signs that health attention is needed?; Are my protective strategies working effectively to maintain wellbeing?; Can I handle complex health situations using multiple strategies?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Guide student to notice one obvious health concern in a simple scenario; Demonstrate one basic protective strategy step by step
feedback_moves_emerging: Help student practice identifying health concerns without prompting; Show how to check if a strategy is working before moving to the next step
feedback_moves_developing: Point out subtle health indicators the student missed; Encourage combining two protective strategies for better effectiveness
feedback_moves_competent: Challenge student with complex scenarios involving multiple health factors; Guide integration of several protective strategies simultaneously
